core_concept: Trade and Exchange: Why People and Communities Trade With Each Other
facts: Trade is the exchange of goods, services, or money between people or communities, not a one-sided giveaway, because no one can produce everything they need alone; for example, a baker needs flour from the miller, and the miller needs bread from the baker.

Specialization means focusing on what you do best; a region with good soil for grapes will grow grapes and trade them for oranges from a region with a warmer climate, rather than trying to grow both poorly.
Barter is trading goods directly for other goods without using money; a fisherman might trade five fish for a pair of boots from a cobbler, but only if each wants what the other has.

When communities trade, both sides gain because they get goods they cannot easily produce themselves; a farming village that grows wheat trades it to a fishing village for fish, and both villages end up with a more varied diet than if they only ate what they produced.
misconceptions: Students believe that if they trade a toy for a book, they have lost the toy and gained something less valuable, not realizing that trade happens only when both parties value what they receive more than what they give up.

Students believe that specialization means you can only do one job forever, so a farmer who specializes in growing wheat cannot also keep cows, even though specialization is about focusing on what you do best, not forbidding other activities.
